## **Appendices** {#appendices}

### **Appendix A. Reproducibility and Execution Notes** {#appendix-a.-reproducibility-and-execution-notes}

**A.1 Execution Environment**

All backbone-specific experiments were executed on Google Colab using an NVIDIA A100 GPU. The language-model backbones were kept frozen throughout the experiments, and only the lightweight hallucination-probe components were trained. Specifically, trainable parameters were limited to the CEV and IAV MLP probes, while calibration parameters and fusion weights were selected using validation data.

The comparison and visualization notebook was used only as a result-aggregation stage. It loaded exported metrics from the backbone-specific runs and generated cross-model tables and figures. It was therefore not treated as a separate model-training experiment.

The final experimental pipeline can be summarized as follows: Frozen LLM backbone → CEV or IAV feature extraction → probe training → temperature calibration → probability-level fusion → closed-loop controller evaluation.

**A.2 Frozen-Backbone Setting**

All evaluated LLMs were used as frozen decoder-only generators. The proposed framework does not fine-tune the LLM parameters. This design choice keeps the system lightweight and makes the method applicable as an inference-time hallucination-control layer.

| Component | Trainable | Function |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| LLM backbone | No | Generates candidate responses |
| Retriever / FAISS index | No | Provides external evidence |
| CEV probe | Yes | Scores residual-stream hallucination risk |
| IAV probe | Yes | Scores FFN-activation hallucination risk |
| Temperature parameters | Tuned | Calibrate probe probabilities |
| Fusion weight | Tuned | Combines CEV and IAV probabilities |
| Controller | No | Applies deterministic threshold policy |

This setting separates hallucination-control training from language-model training. The backbone remains unchanged, while the probing and controller layers provide an additional reliability mechanism around the RAG pipeline.

### **Appendix B. Implementation Details and Memory Management** {#appendix-b.-implementation-details-and-memory-management}

**B.1 Sources of Memory Pressure**

Early implementations exposed several sources of GPU memory pressure. The most common issue came from full forward passes through AutoModelForCausalLM, which can materialize vocabulary-sized logits: batch size × sequence length × vocabulary size.

For large-vocabulary models, this tensor can create a substantial temporary memory spike, especially when combined with hook-captured activations.

Additional sources of memory pressure included:

| Source | Explanation |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Hook tensors remaining on GPU** | Captured activations can accumulate if not detached |
| **Long generated sequences** | Longer sequence lengths increase activation and logit memory |
| **Retriever and LLM co-residency** | Keeping both on GPU increases peak VRAM |
| **Verbose generations** | Long or thinking-style outputs increase sequence length |
| **Unremoved hooks** | Repeated extraction can accumulate stale hook references |

**B.2 Engineering Mitigations**

The final implementation used the following memory-management choices:

| Issue | Mitigation |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Vocabulary-logit memory spike** | Use the base transformer module for feature extraction when possible, avoiding unnecessary lm\_head logits |
| **Hook tensors occupying GPU memory** | Immediately apply .detach().cpu().float() to captured activations |
| **Hook accumulation** | Remove hooks after each extraction pass |
| **Retriever and LLM competing for VRAM** | Move retrieval components to CPU during LLM feature extraction when needed |
| **Long generated sequences** | Apply maximum token caps during evaluation |
| **Degenerate repeated outputs** | Apply a 4-gram repetition detector |
| **Hidden-state scale mismatch** | Normalize extracted features using training-set statistics |

These implementation choices reduce peak memory usage while preserving the frozen-backbone probing setup.

### **Appendix C. Degeneration and Artifact Handling** {#appendix-c.-degeneration-and-artifact-handling}

**C.1 Repetition Guard**

A 4-gram repetition ratio is used to detect degenerate generation loops. It is defined as the fraction of 4-grams that are repeated rather than unique:

r\_4gram \= 1 − ( \#unique 4-grams / \#total 4-grams )

A higher value indicates more repetition. If the repetition ratio exceeds the degeneration threshold, the output is treated as risky and routed toward regeneration or abstention.

**C.2 Model-Specific Output Cleaning**

Some backbones produced generation artifacts that could interfere with output delivery or scoring. Lightweight cleaning rules were applied to user-facing outputs.

| Backbone | Cleaning step |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2** | Remove repetitive artifacts, malformed continuation patterns, and format spillover |
| **Qwen3-8B** | Remove thinking-mode or hidden-reasoning artifacts before final delivery |
| **LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct** | Minimal cleaning required |

These cleaning steps affect artifact handling and final text presentation. They do not replace the internal-activation hallucination scoring mechanism.

### **Appendix D. Unused Learned Policy Component** {#appendix-d.-unused-learned-policy-component}

**D.1 PolicyNetwork Design**

An optional learned policy controller was explored during development but was not used in the final reported experiments.

The proposed policy state was:

s \= ( p\_fused, p\_CEV, p\_IAV, q\_ret, a / N\_max )

where a is the current attempt number and N\_max is the maximum retry count.

The learned policy produced a softmax distribution over: {REGENERATE, RE-RETRIEVE, ABSTAIN}. The ACCEPT decision remained threshold-based.

**D.2 REINFORCE Exploration**

A REINFORCE-style policy-learning experiment was explored during development: RUN\_REINFORCE \= False.

The learned policy was disabled in the final experiments because the rollout setting did not provide a stable reward signal. In early trials, the policy tended to collapse toward a single action rather than learning a balanced policy over regeneration, re-retrieval, and abstention.

The main causes were:

| Cause | Effect |
| :---- | :---- |
| Limited rollout queries | High-variance policy estimates |
| Sparse reward signal | Weak learning signal |
| No direct hallucination ground truth during SQuAD rollouts | Difficult reward assignment |
| Stochastic generation variance | Unstable policy updates |
| Added runtime cost | No clear improvement over deterministic control |

**D.3 Future Use of Learned Policies**

A learned controller may be useful in future work if trained with:

1. Larger rollout budgets.  
2. Human or reference-based feedback.  
3. Explicit rewards for faithfulness, correctness, and utility.  
4. Off-policy training or supervised imitation from successful controller traces.  
5. Separate penalties for unnecessary abstention.

### **Appendix E. Deterministic Controller Pseudocode** {#appendix-e.-deterministic-controller-pseudocode}

The final system uses a deterministic threshold controller.

Input:

  p\_fused      calibrated hallucination-risk probability

  q\_ret        retrieval-quality score

  attempts     current retry count

  theta\_hallu  intervention threshold

  theta\_abs    abstention threshold

  theta\_ret    retrieval-quality threshold

  max\_retries  maximum allowed attempts

if attempts \>= max\_retries:

    return ABSTAIN

if p\_fused \< theta\_hallu:

    return ACCEPT

if p\_fused \>= theta\_abs:

    return ABSTAIN

if q\_ret \< theta\_ret:

    return RE-RETRIEVE

return REGENERATE

The deterministic controller is interpretable, auditable, and directly aligned with the closed-loop RAG action space.
